params.description: Sorting workspace appears. Left side shows three draggable problem cards: (1) 'How many more chose pizza than tacos?', (2) 'How many more chose pizza and tacos combined than salad?', (3) 'How many fewer chose burgers and hot dogs together than pizza?' Right side shows two drop zones labeled 'JUST COMPARE' and 'COMBINE FIRST, THEN COMPARE'.

items.text: How many more chose pizza than tacos?
How many more chose pizza and tacos combined than salad?
How many fewer chose burgers and hot dogs together than pizza?

beats.text: Here's what you've been doing. You look at a problem and decide: Do I just compare? Or do I combine first, then compare? Sort these questions.
Match the task to the group.

validator.description: Student sorted all problems correctly: problem 1 to JUST COMPARE, problems 2 and 3 to COMBINE FIRST

beats.text: You sorted them all. Words like 'combined' and 'together' tell you to find a total first.

elements.description: Sorting workspace with three problem cards draggable to two zones: JUST COMPARE and COMBINE FIRST, THEN COMPARE. Drag-to-sort tool active.

params.description: Sorting area fades. Problem cards are no longer draggable.

beats.text: What question do you ask yourself to decide?

validator.description: Student selected option B, the correct key question

beats.text: That's the key question. The answer tells you whether it's one step or two.

elements.description: Sorting workspace inactive. Multiple choice options visible: 'What's the biggest number?', 'Does it say 'combined' or 'together'?', 'How many categories are there?', 'Should I add or subtract?'

beats.text: This is strategic thinking. You're not just doing math. You're planning your approach based on what the problem says.

elements.description: Sorting workspace remains on screen, interaction complete.
